title: Tabbed settings can hide auto-post exfiltration config
description: This commit changes the settings screen from a single page where all settings were rendered to a tabbed UI that always opens on the Output tab. Sensitive settings such as the main Discord webhook URL, VRChat photo auto-post enablement/webhook, and screenshot auto-post enablement/webhook are now hidden behind separate tabs with no summary or warning on the default tab. An attacker can provide a config.json that enables autoPhoto or screenshotAutoPost and points the webhook to an attacker-controlled Discord webhook. If the user opens that config and clicks Save from the default Output tab, the full hidden config is persisted and saveConfigLocked restarts the auto-photo watchers. Those watchers force Discord upload and use the configured webhook, causing future VRChat photos or screenshots in the watched folders to be sent to the attacker-controlled Discord channel. The backend already supports this functionality, but this commit removes meaningful first-screen visibility for the sensitive imported settings.

title: Release build trusts cached Wails executable
description: The release workflow now places ~/go/bin on PATH, restores ~/go/bin/wails.exe from GitHub Actions cache, and skips `go install github.com/wailsapp/wails/v2/cmd/wails@${{ env.WAILS_VERSION }}` whenever the cache key hits. The cached binary is then executed for `wails version` and later used to build the release executable. The cache key only includes runner OS, Go version, and Wails version; it does not authenticate the cached executable or compare it with a known digest. `wails version` is not an integrity check because a malicious binary can print the expected version. If an attacker can populate or poison the cache in a scope visible to the release workflow, the release job will execute attacker-controlled code and can produce a backdoored Windows executable that is subsequently packaged and signed. Release workflows should avoid trusting cached executables, or should reinstall/rebuild the CLI in the release job and verify an expected cryptographic digest before execution.

title: Tag-name shell injection exposes release signing secrets
description: The commit adds Bash commands that copy signature and public-key artifacts to filenames containing `${{ github.ref_name }}`. Because GitHub Actions expression interpolation happens before the shell runs, a tag name containing shell metacharacters such as a double quote and semicolon can break out of the quoted destination path and execute arbitrary Bash commands. This step has `CI_RELEASE_GPG_PRIVATE_KEY`, `CI_RELEASE_GPG_PASSPHRASE`, and `CI_RELEASE_GPG_FINGERPRINT` in the environment, has already imported the private key into `GNUPGHOME`, and runs before the final zip packaging. An attacker able to trigger this release workflow with a crafted tag name could exfiltrate the release signing key/passphrase or modify and re-sign packaged artifacts.

title: PGP verification trusts a release-bundled public key
description: The new release workflow exports `ClipForVRChat-release-signing-public-key.asc` as another release asset, and both README/UI guidance instruct users to import that key from the same release before verifying `ClipForVRChat.exe.asc`. The UI further states that seeing `Good signature` means the executable is confirmed as official. This lacks a trust anchor: users are not given an out-of-band fingerprint, embedded/pinned public key, or other independent way to confirm that the imported key is the project's real release key. Therefore, compromise of the release assets, a malicious mirror, or any substituted download bundle can bypass the intended signature check by replacing the exe, asc, and public key together.

title: GitHub Actions tag name command injection
description: The release job runs on tag pushes matching `v*` and grants `contents: write`. This commit adds a `Prepare version` PowerShell step that embeds `${{ github.ref_name }}` directly into a double-quoted PowerShell string, then uses the resulting output in another PowerShell command. Git tag names can contain characters such as double quotes, semicolons, `#`, and redirection characters, so a tag such as `v";whoami;#` can break out of the string and execute arbitrary PowerShell on the release runner. Because this job builds and publishes release artifacts with a write-scoped GitHub token, command execution here can tamper with official release assets. The workflow also still contains older direct `${{ github.ref_name }}` interpolations in packaging paths, so the tag name should be validated or passed through environment variables and treated strictly as data.

title: Unpinned govulncheck runs in release workflow
description: The new Go vulnerability check is executed as `go run golang.org/x/vuln/cmd/govulncheck@latest ./...` in the release workflow. Because `@latest` is resolved at workflow runtime rather than being pinned in the repository, a compromised or malicious future `golang.org/x/vuln` release would execute arbitrary code on the GitHub Actions runner. In the release job, this occurs with `contents: write` permission and before the application is built, packaged, hashed, and uploaded. That malicious code could modify source files, alter the built executable, replace release artifacts, or abuse persisted checkout credentials/release permissions. This is a supply-chain release-integrity issue introduced by the commit. Pinning govulncheck to a reviewed version, tracking it through a tools module/lockfile, and reducing permissions until the publish step would reduce the risk.

title: Imported configs can start silent auto-photo exfiltration
description: This commit adds AutoPhoto settings and starts the watcher unconditionally from App.startup whenever cfg.AutoPhoto.Enabled is true. The existing startup path allows a single .json argument to be loaded into settings mode for editing/review. Because startup calls restartAutoPhotoWatcher regardless of the current UI mode or whether the config has been saved/confirmed, an attacker can supply a JSON config with autoPhoto.enabled=true, autoPhoto.photoDirectory set to a sensitive or broad image directory, and autoPhoto.webhookUrl or discord.webhookUrl set to an attacker-controlled Discord webhook or compatible upload endpoint. Once the UI opens, the watcher recursively scans the configured directory and processes newly appearing image files. The process path forcibly enables Discord upload and substitutes the auto-photo webhook if present, so future VRChat photos or other newly created supported images are automatically uploaded without an explicit processing action by the user. Existing files are marked as seen on watcher startup, so this primarily affects new files created after opening the config, but it still violates user consent and can leak private photos/paths while the user is merely reviewing settings.

title: Release workflow injects untrusted tag names into PowerShell
description: This commit adds version injection and versioned artifact names by embedding `${{ github.ref_name }}` directly inside PowerShell `run` commands. Git ref names are attacker-controlled input for tag-triggered workflows and can contain shell metacharacters such as quotes, semicolons, `$()`, and `#` while still matching the `v*` tag trigger. For example, a tag name such as `v";whoami;#` would turn the build line into a PowerShell command sequence and execute `whoami` on the runner. The same pattern is present in the `Compress-Archive` destination path. Because this workflow has `contents: write` and publishes release assets, a user who can create a matching tag could execute commands during release, tamper with built artifacts, and publish malicious binaries without changing reviewed source code. The tag value should be validated against an expected release-version pattern such as `^v[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+$`, passed via environment variables rather than directly interpolated into shell, and separately sanitized before use in file names.

title: Release workflow uses unpinned write-capable actions
description: The release workflow introduced in this commit runs on release tags and manual dispatch with `contents: write`, then executes `softprops/action-gh-release@v2`. Because the action is referenced by a mutable tag rather than an immutable commit SHA, a compromise of the upstream action repository, action maintainer account, or tag could cause arbitrary code to run in this repository's release workflow with a write-capable GITHUB_TOKEN. That code could create or modify official GitHub releases and upload malicious release assets under the project's trusted release channel. `actions/checkout@v4` is also tag-pinned, but the most security-relevant issue is the third-party release action combined with write permissions.
